title: Shepherds, Humility, and Standing Firm
subtitle: Elders shepherding, younger submitting, all clothed with humility against the devil

heb.paragraph: I exhort the elders among you - presbuteroi here denotes church leaders, not merely older men. Peter appeals as a fellow elder.
Shepherd the flock of God - the pastoral metaphor dominates. Elders are under-shepherds; Christ is the chief Shepherd.
Not domineering over those in your charge - leadership is service, not tyranny. Elders are examples, not overlords.

refs.note: Feed my lambs. Tend my sheep. Feed my sheep.
Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock.
Woe to the shepherds of Israel who have been feeding themselves!
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

notes.note: Peter exhorts elders as a fellow elder and witness of Christ's sufferings. Shepherd the flock of God, exercising oversight willingly, eagerly.
Not domineering but being examples. When the chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory.
Younger ones, submit to elders. All of you, clothe yourselves with humility. God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
Calvin emphasizes that elders are under-shepherds, not owners. The flock belongs to God; leaders are accountable to the chief Shepherd.
Humility is clothing - an outer garment that should be visible. Pride is the root sin; humility the root virtue.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The three contrasts define good leadership: willing not compelled, eager not greedy, exemplary not domineering.
The unfading crown (amarantinos stephanos) contrasts with the fading wreaths given to athletes. This reward endures.
Peter quotes Proverbs 3:34 LXX. The opposition of God to the proud is active; he resists them.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that Peter's self-designation as fellow elder is remarkable. The apostle identifies with local church leaders.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes observes that the shepherd image connects to 2:25 where Christ is the Shepherd. Human shepherds follow his pattern.

hist.historical: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) — Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. These believers face social ostracism and verbal abuse (4:4, 14) rather than official state persecution. As 'elect exiles' (1:1), they experience the marginalization of those who no longer participate in pagan civic religion, trade guild feasts, and family worship. The letter's household codes (2:13–3:7) address how Christians should live within Roman social structures. Peter writes from 'Babylon' (5:13) — almost certainly a symbolic name for Rome. Silvanus (Silas) serves as secretary (5:12), and Mark is present (5:13). The letter likely dates to the early 60s AD, before Nero's persecution.
hist.context: Peter, fellow elder and witness of Christ's sufferings, exhorts elders to shepherd God's flock. Oversight must be willing, not forced; eager, not greedy; exemplary, not domineering. When the chief Shepherd appears, they will receive the unfading crown of glory. Younger ones are to submit to elders. All are to clothe themselves with humility toward one another, for God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

heb.paragraph: Humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God - active self-humbling is commanded. God will exalt at the proper time.
Be sober-minded, be watchful - spiritual alertness is necessary because an adversary prowls.
Resist him, firm in your faith - the devil is resisted by faith, not by special techniques. Firmness defeats his attacks.

refs.note: Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.
Put on the whole armor of God, that you may stand against the schemes of the devil.
Cast your burden on the LORD, and he will sustain you.
The sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory to be revealed.

notes.note: Humble yourselves under God's mighty hand so that he may exalt you at the proper time. Cast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you.
Be sober, be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour. Resist him, firm in faith.
After you have suffered a little while, the God of all grace will restore, confirm, strengthen, establish you. To him be dominion forever.
Calvin connects humility with casting anxiety. The humble recognize their dependence on God and entrust their cares to him.
The lion imagery emphasizes danger. Satan is powerful but not omnipotent. Firm faith defeats him.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The participle casting is dependent on humble yourselves. Casting anxiety is an expression of humility - trusting God rather than self.
The roaring lion seeks to devour - the verb is violent. Satan's intent is destruction, not mere annoyance.
The four verbs - restore, confirm, strengthen, establish - are future indicatives functioning as promises. God will act.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that suffering is not meaningless. It is brief (a little while) and purposeful (God will restore). The doxology affirms God's sovereignty.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes observes that the worldwide brotherhood (v. 9) reminds isolated Christians they are not alone. Suffering is shared.

hist.context: The exhortation continues: humble yourselves so that God may exalt you at the right time. Cast all anxiety on him because he cares. Be sober; the devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking prey. Resist him, firm in faith, knowing that the same sufferings are experienced by the worldwide brotherhood. After suffering a little while, the God of all grace will restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish. To him be dominion forever.

heb.paragraph: I have testified that this is the true grace of God - Peter summarizes the letter's message: this is genuine grace; stand firm in it.
She who is at Babylon - almost certainly a symbolic reference to Rome, the capital of empire as Babylon was in its day.
Greet one another with a kiss of love. Peace to all who are in Christ.

refs.note: Judas called Barsabbas, and Silas, leading men among the brothers.
Get Mark and bring him with you, for he is very useful to me.
Babylon the great, mother of prostitutes.
Greet one another with a holy kiss.

notes.note: By Silvanus I have written to you briefly, exhorting and declaring that this is the true grace of God. Stand firm in it.
She who is at Babylon sends greetings, as does Mark my son. Greet one another with the kiss of love. Peace to all in Christ.
Calvin identifies Babylon as Rome. The church in the imperial capital greets the churches in Asia Minor. Mark is likely John Mark.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Silvanus (Silas) is described as a faithful brother. He may have served as secretary or courier or both.
Babylon is almost universally understood as a cryptic reference to Rome. Early Christians avoided naming Rome directly in correspondence.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner sees the letter summary in v. 12: this is the true grace of God. Everything in the letter - suffering, hope, holiness - is grace.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes that the mention of Mark connects Peter to early Gospel traditions. Peter and Mark are associated in early church testimony.

hist.context: Peter concludes with personal notes. Silvanus (Silas) is the letter carrier and likely amanuensis. The letter's purpose is summarized: exhortation and testimony that this is the true grace of God - stand firm in it. Greetings come from the chosen sister in Babylon (Rome) and from Mark, described as Peter's son. The kiss of love and the blessing of peace close the letter.

themes.note: Faith is central: resist the devil firm in faith. Sovereignty appears in God's mighty hand and the promise of restoration.

lit.note: Chapter 5 addresses church leadership, spiritual warfare, and concludes with personal greetings from Babylon (Rome).

discourse.thesis: Shepherd the flock of God — humble yourselves, resist the devil, and the God of all grace will restore you.

nodes.text: Peter exhorts elders as a fellow elder and witness of Christ's sufferings: shepherd God's flock among you, exercising oversight willingly, not for shameful gain, not domineering — being examples. When the chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory.

Likewise, you who are younger, be subject to elders. Clothe yourselves, all of you, with humility — God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble. Humble yourselves under God's mighty hand; cast all your anxieties on him.

Be sober-minded, watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion. Resist him, firm in faith — the same sufferings are experienced by your brotherhood worldwide. The God of all grace will himself restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish you.

By Silvanus, Peter writes briefly, exhorting and declaring this is the true grace of God — stand firm in it. The church in Babylon sends greetings, and so does Mark. Greet one another with a kiss of love. Peace to all who are in Christ.

coaching.tip: 'Shepherd the flock of God that is among you, exercising oversight, not under compulsion but willingly' (v. 2). Peter — himself commissioned to 'feed my sheep' (John 21:15-17) — commissions elders. The motivation matters: willingly, eagerly, as examples. Leadership style flows from inner disposition. Reluctant shepherds produce wandering sheep.

'Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' (v. 8). After addressing suffering, Peter names the adversary. Resistance is communal: 'Resist him, firm in your faith, knowing that the same kinds of suffering are being experienced by your brotherhood throughout the world' (v. 9). You're not alone in the fight.
